channel.title: Study Notes on This and That
channel.description: Recent content in Study Notes on This and That

item.title: Introduction to Machine Learning
item.description:
Machine Learning (ML)

Supervised machine learning - combine input to produce useful predictions on new data Unsupervised machine learning

Key Terminology

lable: the variable we are predicting (y) ex: house price, sales

feature: input variable (x) ex: sender’s email address, time, words

  • Useful features are quantifiable.
  • We train the model with labeled examples and predict the lable on unlabled data.

model: system that defines the relationship between features and lable training: creating or learning the model inference: appling the trained model to unlabled examples => use the model to predict y

regression - for continuous values ex: what is the price? classification - for discrete values ex: is it spam or not spam?

L{2} loss

= squared loss sum of (y - prediction(x))^2

Linear Regression

By convention in machine learning, equation for a model is slightly different:

y′=b+w1x1

where:

  • y′ is the predicted label (a desired output).
  • b is the bias (the y-intercept), sometimes referred to as w0.
  • w1 is the weight of feature 1. Weight is the same concept as the “slope” m in the traditional equation of a line.
  • x1 is a feature (a known input).

Training and Loss

GOAL of training is minimize loss.

empirical risk minimization: the process which tries to minimize loss by examining a lot of examples.

Mean square error (MSE) is the average squared loss per example over the whole dataset. To calculate MSE, sum up all the squared losses for individual examples and then divide by the number of examples:

MSE=1N∑(x,y)∈D(y−prediction(x))2

where:

  • (x,y) is an example in which
    • x is the set of features (ex: minute, age, gender) that the model uses to make predictions.
    • y is the example’s label (ex: example, temperature).
  • prediction(x) is a function of the weights and bias in combination with the set of features x.
  • D is a data set containing many labeled examples, which are (x,y) pairs.
  • N is the number of examples in D.

item.title: Sampling
item.description:
Reasons to sample:

obtaining & processing the entire data is:

  1. expensive
  2. time consuming

Types of sampling

  1. simple random sampling
  2. sampling with/without replacement
  3. stratified sampling
  4. etc
  • Using a sample will work IF the sample is REPRESENTATIVE OF THE POPULATION
  • a sample is representative

item.title: Linear Regression
item.description:
The simplest model that we can fit to data is a line. When we are trying to find a line that fits a set of data best, we are performing Linear Regression.

A line is determined by its slope and its intercept. In other words, for each point y on a line we can say:

y = m x + by=mx+b

where m is the slope, and b is the intercept. y is a given point on the y-axis, and it corresponds to a given x on the x-axis.

The goal is to get the “best” m and b for our data to find the best fit model.

For each data point, we calculate loss, a number that measures how bad the model’s prediction was. This loss is calculated as the squared distance from the point to the line. The best m and b is the values that minimize the loss on average across all of the data.

Gradient Descent

As we try to minimize loss, we take each parameter we are changing, and move it as long as we are decreasing loss. The process by which we do this is called gradient descent. We move in the direction that decreases our loss the most. Gradient refers to the slope of the curve at any point.

For example, let’s say we are trying to find the intercept for a line. We currently have a guess of 10 for the intercept. At the point of 10 on the curve, the slope is downward. Therefore, if we increase the intercept, we should be lowering the loss. So we follow the gradient downwards.

Wrap Up!

  • We can measure how well a line fits by measuring loss.
  • The goal of linear regression is to minimize loss.
  • To find the line of best fit, we try to find the b value (intercept) and the m value (slope) that minimize loss.
  • Convergence refers to when the parameters stop changing with each iteration.
  • Learning rate refers to how much the parameters are changed on each iteration.
  • We can use Scikit-learn’s LinearRegression() model to perform linear regression on a set of points.

These are important tools to have in your toolkit as you continue your exploration of data science.
